transcript:
Interviewer: Priya, sustainable fashion has been a topic for years now. Where are we really at?

Priya Kapoor: We're at an interesting inflection point. The awareness phase is largely over — most consumers know that fast fashion is problematic. But there's a massive gap between awareness and action. Our reader surveys show that 78% of our audience say sustainability influences their purchasing decisions, but when we look at actual behaviour, fast fashion sales are still growing. So the challenge for the industry — and for advertisers — is bridging that intention-action gap.

Interviewer: What's driving that gap?

Priya Kapoor: Price is the obvious one, but it's more nuanced than that. The sustainable fashion space has a communication problem. Too much messaging is guilt-based — 'your wardrobe is destroying the planet' — which creates cognitive dissonance but not behaviour change. The brands making real progress are the ones reframing sustainability as aspiration rather than sacrifice. Patagonia has done this brilliantly with their 'Buy Less, Demand More' positioning. It's not 'don't buy'; it's 'buy better'.

Interviewer: How does this translate to content at Conscious Living?

Priya Kapoor: We've shifted from 'sustainable alternatives' roundups to storytelling about the people and processes behind sustainable brands. Our readers engage three times more with a profile of a weaver in a fair-trade cooperative than with a list of eco-friendly t-shirts. The human story creates emotional connection that product features can't match.

Interviewer: What about the circular economy? Is that resonating with consumers?

Priya Kapoor: Hugely, and this is where I see the biggest commercial opportunity. Resale, rental, and repair are no longer fringe behaviours — they're mainstream. Vestiaire Collective and Vinted have made second-hand fashion aspirational. Rental platforms like HURR are growing rapidly. And repair services — brands like Nudie Jeans offering free repairs for life — are building incredible brand loyalty.

Interviewer: What's the demographic split on this?

Priya Kapoor: It's fascinating. Gen Z are the most vocal about sustainability but also the biggest consumers of Shein and fast fashion. The demographic actually spending money on sustainable fashion is predominantly 30-50, higher income, often parents who are thinking about the world they're leaving for their children. That's the sweet spot for advertisers in this space.

Interviewer: Any verticals or angles that are underexplored?

Priya Kapoor: Corporate workwear. There's been loads of innovation in casual sustainable fashion but almost nothing for professional settings. The capsule wardrobe concept is tailor-made for workwear — five to seven high-quality, versatile pieces that mix and match. A brand that cracked sustainable professional clothing with modern styling would own a massive, affluent market segment. Also, sustainable fashion for plus sizes — the intersection of size inclusivity and sustainability is barely addressed and there's strong demand.

Interviewer: What messaging works for advertisers?

Priya Kapoor: Focus on quality and longevity, not on 'saving the planet'. The most effective messaging we see is around cost-per-wear: 'This £200 coat will last ten years, so it's £20 per year versus a £40 coat you'll replace annually.' That reframes the price premium as an investment. Also, transparency about supply chains builds enormous trust — show the factory, name the workers, trace the materials. Consumers are sophisticated enough to spot vague claims, and they reward brands that are genuinely open about their processes.

Interviewer: Final thoughts?

Priya Kapoor: The brands that will win in sustainable fashion are the ones that make sustainability invisible — it should be a given, not a selling point. Just like organic food has become mainstream, sustainable fashion needs to reach a point where it's simply how clothes are made. The advertiser opportunity right now is to be one of the brands that helps normalise that transition.
